text:
AI Developer Radar｜Daily｜2026-06-20

今日结论：
The dominant pattern today is sustained high attention on AI Agent Frameworks and Coding Agents, with GLM-5 (487 daily stars) and SWE-agent (3,546 avg daily delta) leading. MCP/tool-calling projects show extreme weekly acceleration (avg 5,646 stars/week), indicating growing developer interest in agent-tool integration. Meanwhile, RAG/knowledge base projects like siyuan-note/siyuan and stas00/ml-engineering show sudden breakout acceleration (2.6x), suggesting a niche but sharp spike. The absence of new multi-source crossovers (0 cross-source projects) and quiet Product Hunt launches suggests attention is concentrated on GitHub rather than broader launch platforms. If this trend continues, we may see more specialized agent frameworks emerge, particularly those combining long-context LLMs with tool-use capabilities.

与昨日对比：
  + 新进 Top 10: zai-org/GLM-5, Kong/insomnia, Lightricks/LTX-2
  - 退出 Top 10: aishwaryanr/awesome-generative-ai-guide, milvus-io/milvus, DeusData/codebase-memory-mcp
  方向变化: Other AI -> AI Agent Framework

今日精选项目
1. zai-org/GLM-5
   GitHub: https://github.com/zai-org/GLM-5
   Category: AI Agent Framework
   Stars: 4,448 (24h +487, 7d 暂无基线)
   Trend: 持续热门 | Acceleration: 1x (low, 前 3 日均值 n/a)
   Score: 64 | Risk: Low
   One-liner: GLM-5 is a family of open-source large language models (744B parameters, 40B active) optimized for long-horizon agentic coding and systems engineering, with the latest GLM-5.2 achieving solid 1M-token context and top open-source scores on Terminal-Bench and SWE-bench.
   Why worth watching: The repo has 4,448 stars and gained 487 stars in the last 24 hours, with a sustained_hot trend and low acceleration (1.0), indicating steady, organic growth rather than a sudden spike. The project is actively maintained (commits within 7 days) and has strong community signals (Discord, WeChat).
   Developer takeaway: This signals a shift toward open-source models that can sustain complex, multi-step reasoning over long horizons, reducing reliance on expensive closed APIs. The IndexShare architecture and asynchronous RL infrastructure (slime) are innovations worth watching for efficient long-context deployment.
   Problem solved: Existing LLMs plateau on complex, multi-step software engineering tasks that require sustained reasoning over hundreds of tool calls and long contexts. GLM-5 series addresses this with improved architecture (IndexShare sparse attention, asynchronous RL infrastructure slime) and training that maintains effectiveness over extended agentic sessions.
   What changed: No single obvious trigger — the project appears to be sustaining organic growth. The recent release of GLM-5.2 (with 1M context and improved benchmarks) and the associated blog/technical report likely maintain ongoing interest.
   Target users: AI engineers building autonomous coding agents, researchers in long-context LLMs, and teams needing open-source models for complex software engineering tasks (repo generation, terminal automation, long-horizon planning).
   Risk notes: The model is very large (744B parameters, 40B active), requiring significant compute for local deployment. The README is promotional and may overstate benchmark results; independent verification is needed. No recent release tag found, so versioning may be unclear.
   LLM confidence: high

2. Kong/insomnia
   GitHub: https://github.com/Kong/insomnia
   Category: API Development & Testing
   Stars: 38,886 (24h +314, 7d 暂无基线)
   Trend: 持续热门 | Acceleration: 1x (low, 前 3 日均值 n/a)
   Score: 58 | Risk: Low
   One-liner: Insomnia is an open-source, cross-platform API client supporting GraphQL, REST, WebSockets, SSE, and gRPC with local, Git, and cloud storage.
   Why worth watching: With 38,886 stars and a 24-hour delta of +314, Insomnia shows strong, sustained community interest. The acceleration ratio is low (1), indicating steady organic growth rather than a sudden spike. This consistent attention signals a reliable, widely adopted tool in the API ecosystem.
   Developer takeaway: For AI developers, Insomnia's support for GraphQL and gRPC aligns with modern API patterns used in AI services. Its plugin ecosystem and CLI enable integration into CI/CD pipelines, making it a practical choice for testing AI model endpoints.
   Problem solved: Developers need a single, powerful tool to design, debug, test, mock, and collaborate on APIs across multiple protocols without switching between different clients or services.
   What changed: No single obvious trigger — the project appears to be sustaining organic growth. Recent commits and a release within 30 days suggest ongoing maintenance and feature improvements, but no specific event (e.g., major version launch or viral post) is evident from the metadata.
   Target users: API developers, backend engineers, frontend developers, QA engineers, and teams needing collaborative API design, testing, and documentation.
   Risk notes: The project requires an account for most features (though local scratch pad is account-free), which may concern privacy-focused users. Competition from Postman and other clients is strong, but Insomnia's open-source model is a differentiator.
   LLM confidence: high

3. Lightricks/LTX-2
   GitHub: https://github.com/Lightricks/LTX-2
   Category: Generative AI / Video Generation
   Stars: 7,622 (24h +197, 7d 暂无基线)
   Trend: 持续热门 | Acceleration: 1x (low, 前 3 日均值 n/a)
   Score: 58 | Risk: Low
   One-liner: LTX-2 is an open-source DiT-based audio-video foundation model from Lightricks that generates synchronized video and audio with high fidelity, offering multiple inference pipelines and LoRA training.
   Why worth watching: With 7,622 stars, a 24-hour star delta of +197, and sustained commit activity (last push June 17, 2026), the project is experiencing steady, high-velocity growth. The acceleration ratio of 1 indicates a consistent upward trend rather than a sudden spike, suggesting organic community adoption and ongoing development.
   Developer takeaway: This signals a shift toward unified multimodal generation models that reduce pipeline complexity. For AI developers, LTX-2 represents an opportunity to integrate state-of-the-art video+audio generation into products without stitching together separate components, potentially lowering the barrier for video AI applications.
   Problem solved: It provides a single model capable of generating synchronized audio and video, eliminating the need to combine separate models for video generation and audio synchronization, while offering production-ready outputs and open access.
   What changed: No single obvious trigger — the project appears to be sustaining organic growth. Recent commits and the release of LTX-2.3 model checkpoints (including distilled and upscaler variants) likely maintain developer interest.
   Target users: AI researchers, video content creators, developers building generative media tools, and companies needing automated video production with synchronized audio.
   Risk notes: The model requires significant VRAM (22B parameters) and multiple model downloads (checkpoints, upscalers, LoRAs), which may limit deployment on consumer hardware. The repository is under active development, so APIs and pipelines may change. No recent official release tag suggests potential instability.
   LLM confidence: high

4. siyuan-note/siyuan
   GitHub: https://github.com/siyuan-note/siyuan
   Category: RAG / Knowledge Base
   Stars: 44,517 (24h +29, 7d +113)
   Trend: 突然爆火 | Acceleration: 2.6x (high, 前 3 日均值 11.3)
   Score: 62 | Risk: Low
   One-liner: A privacy-first, self-hosted personal knowledge management system with block-level references and Markdown WYSIWYG, built with TypeScript and Go.
   Why worth watching: The repo has 44,517 stars with a sudden acceleration of 2.6 (high) and a 24h star delta of 29. The 7-day average star delta is 16.1, but the 3-day average is 11.3, indicating a recent spike in attention. This breakout suggests a new wave of interest, possibly driven by a recent release or community event.
   Developer takeaway: For AI developers, SiYuan's OpenAI API integration and block-level data model offer a foundation for building custom RAG pipelines or AI-assisted writing tools on top of a local-first knowledge base.
   Problem solved: Provides a fully open-source, local-first alternative to proprietary note-taking tools like Notion or Obsidian, with fine-grained block-level linking, two-way links, and self-hosted sync via WebDAV or S3.
   What changed: No single obvious trigger — the project appears to be sustaining organic growth. However, the high acceleration and recent release (within 30 days) suggest that a new version or feature announcement may have catalyzed the surge.
   Target users: Privacy-conscious knowledge workers, researchers, note-takers, and developers who want full control over their data and prefer open-source software.
   Risk notes: Some features (e.g., cloud sync) are paid; the AGPLv3 license may restrict commercial embedding. The project is large and complex, with a steep learning curve for contributors.
   LLM confidence: high

   GitHub: https://github.com/stas00/ml-engineering
   Category: RAG / Knowledge Base
   Stars: 18,144 (24h +12, 7d +57)

   Score: 38 | Risk: Medium
   One-liner: A comprehensive open book of methodologies, tools, and commands for training and fine-tuning large language and multi-modal models.
   Why worth watching: With 18,015 stars and a sudden acceleration ratio of 2.9 (high), the repo is experiencing a breakout. The 24-hour star delta of 20 and 7-day average of 6 indicate a sharp increase in attention, likely driven by the growing need for hands-on ML engineering guidance as LLM training becomes more accessible.
   Developer takeaway: Signals a shift toward open, community-driven knowledge sharing in ML engineering, reducing the barrier for smaller teams to adopt large-scale training practices.
   Problem solved: Provides a centralized, practical reference for ML engineers tackling the complexities of large-scale model training, debugging, and inference, covering hardware, orchestration, and development workflows.
   What changed: No single obvious trigger — the project appears to be sustaining organic growth. However, the recent push on 2026-05-18 and a talk on 2026-01-10 may have contributed to renewed interest.
   Target users: ML engineers, LLM/VLM training operators, and researchers working on large-scale model training and inference.
   Risk notes: The content is based on the author's personal experience and may not cover all edge cases; users should validate scripts for their specific environments.
   LLM confidence: high

6. thedaviddias/Front-End-Checklist
   GitHub: https://github.com/thedaviddias/Front-End-Checklist
   Category: AI Agent Framework
   Stars: 72,973 (24h +16, 7d +75)
   Trend: 突然爆火 | Acceleration: 2.5x (high, 前 3 日均值 6.3)
   Score: 50 | Risk: Low
   One-liner: A comprehensive, MCP-powered front-end quality checklist with 385 rules across 11 categories, designed for both human developers and AI agents.
   Why worth watching: With 72,971 stars, a 24-hour star delta of 14, and a high acceleration of 2.2, this project is experiencing a sudden breakout in attention. The recent release and active commits (within 7 days) indicate ongoing development, making it a timely resource for developers adopting AI-assisted workflows.
   Developer takeaway: This signals a growing trend of turning static documentation into machine-consumable, agent-friendly formats. For AI developers, it demonstrates how to package domain knowledge (e.g., front-end rules) as MCP tools, enabling agents to perform structured, reliable code reviews without hallucination.
   Problem solved: Provides a structured, actionable front-end best practices checklist that can be used interactively on the web, via MCP tools for AI agents, or as a static README, ensuring consistent code quality and compliance across projects.
   What changed: The project recently added MCP server support and a skills system, enabling AI agents to directly use the checklist for code review and audits. This shift from a static checklist to an interactive, agent-compatible tool likely drove the sudden spike in interest.
   Target users: Front-end developers, web developers, QA engineers, and AI/ML engineers building agent-based code review or auditing tools.
   Risk notes: The checklist is opinionated and may not cover all edge cases or specific framework nuances. Over-reliance on automated checks could miss context-specific issues. The MCP server is hosted externally, so availability and latency depend on the provider.
   LLM confidence: high

7. topoteretes/cognee
   GitHub: https://github.com/topoteretes/cognee
   Category: AI Agent Framework
   Stars: 17,905 (24h +44, 7d +106)
   Trend: 突然爆火 | Acceleration: 2.3x (high, 前 3 日均值 19.3)
   Score: 70 | Risk: Low
   One-liner: Cognee is an open-source AI memory platform that gives agents persistent long-term memory via a self-hosted knowledge graph.
   Why worth watching: Cognee is experiencing a sudden breakout with 44 stars in the last 24 hours and a 7-day average of 18.6 stars/day, totaling 17,905 stars. The acceleration ratio of 2.3 indicates a sharp increase in attention momentum, likely driven by recent releases and growing interest in persistent memory for AI agents.
   Developer takeaway: This signals a shift toward memory-first agent architectures. Developers should consider Cognee as a foundational component for building agents that learn and adapt over time, reducing the need for custom memory solutions.
   Problem solved: AI agents lack persistent memory across sessions, forcing them to re-learn context each time. Cognee solves this by ingesting data in any format, building a knowledge graph, and enabling recall, connection, and action with full context.
   What changed: The project has a recent release within 30 days and recent commits within 7 days. The README highlights integrations with Claude Code and OpenClaw, a research paper on arXiv, and new deployment options (Modal, Railway, Fly.io). These updates likely fueled the breakout.
   Target users: AI developers building agents that require persistent context, such as customer support bots, coding assistants, and knowledge management systems.
   Risk notes: The project is still evolving (125 open issues). Self-hosting may require operational overhead. Dependence on LLM providers for core functionality could introduce latency or cost.
   LLM confidence: high

8. chopratejas/headroom
   GitHub: https://github.com/chopratejas/headroom
   Category: Coding Agent / SWE Agent
   Stars: 37,698 (24h +6033, 7d +23035)
   Trend: 突然爆火 | Acceleration: 4.7x (high, 前 3 日均值 1281)
   Score: 95 | Risk: Low
   One-liner: Headroom compresses tool outputs, logs, and RAG chunks by 60-95% before they reach the LLM, preserving answer quality while slashing token costs.
   Why worth watching: Headroom is exploding in popularity: +6,033 stars in 24 hours, +23,035 in 7 days, with a 7-day average of 3,400 stars/day and an acceleration ratio of 4.7 (high). At 37,698 total stars, it's one of the fastest-growing AI infrastructure projects on GitHub. The sudden breakout suggests a viral moment—likely driven by the release of output token reduction, cross-agent memory, or the MCP server capabilities.
   Developer takeaway: This signals a shift toward 'context engineering' as a first-class concern in AI development. Token optimization is no longer an afterthought—it's becoming a core infrastructure layer. Headroom's approach (content-aware compression, reversible caching, output shaping) points to a future where LLM interactions are aggressively optimized for cost and latency without sacrificing quality.
   Also on: GitHub
   Problem solved: LLM context windows fill up quickly with verbose tool outputs, logs, and RAG results, driving up costs and limiting context. Headroom compresses this data losslessly (or near-losslessly) before sending to the LLM, reducing token usage by 60-95% without degrading answer quality.
   What changed: No single obvious trigger — the project appears to be sustaining organic growth. However, the recent addition of output token reduction (cutting what the model writes back), cross-agent memory, and the MCP server likely contributed to the breakout. The project also has a recent release within 30 days and active commits.
   Target users: AI engineers, ML engineers, and developers using LLM-based coding agents (Claude Code, Cursor, Codex, Aider, Copilot), RAG pipelines, or any application that sends large prompts to LLMs. Also relevant for teams building AI agents that process logs, tool outputs, or large files.
   Risk notes: The project is very new (37k stars but likely <6 months old) and may have undiscovered edge cases or bugs. Compression algorithms may not preserve accuracy for all content types (e.g., highly structured data, code with subtle semantics). The 'reversible' compression relies on local caching, which could be a failure point. The sudden breakout may attract maintainability challenges.
   LLM confidence: high

9. Panniantong/Agent-Reach
   GitHub: https://github.com/Panniantong/Agent-Reach
   Category: MCP / Tool Calling
   Stars: 34,971 (24h +1717, 7d +8531)
   Trend: 突然爆火 | Acceleration: 1.2x (high, 前 3 日均值 1425.3)
   Score: 85 | Risk: Low
   One-liner: Agent Reach is a CLI tool that gives AI agents zero-config access to read and search Twitter, Reddit, YouTube, GitHub, Bilibili, Xiaohongshu, and more — all free, no API fees.
   Why worth watching: The repo exploded from 31,787 stars with a 24h delta of +2,809 and a 7-day delta of +7,284. The acceleration ratio is 3.3 (high), indicating a sudden breakout. This is a massive spike in attention, likely driven by viral sharing in AI developer communities.
   Developer takeaway: This signals a shift toward 'capability layers' that abstract away the complexity of platform-specific access. Instead of each agent reinventing the wheel for web scraping, a shared open-source layer can handle authentication, backend selection, and fallback routing. This pattern may become standard for agent infrastructure.
   Also on: GitHub
   Problem solved: AI agents lack native ability to read web content from platforms that require authentication, paid APIs, or complex scraping setups. Agent Reach solves this by providing a unified installer and channel router that selects the best free backend for each platform, handles authentication, and lets agents access content via simple commands.
   What changed: No single obvious trigger — the project appears to be sustaining organic growth. The README is comprehensive and was recently updated (pushed 2026-06-16). The project has a recent release and active commits within 7 days. The breakout may be fueled by word-of-mouth among Claude Code and Cursor users who need free web access for their agents.
   Target users: AI agent developers and power users who use Claude Code, Cursor, OpenClaw, Windsurf, or similar tools and need their agents to read content from Twitter, Reddit, YouTube, GitHub, Bilibili, Xiaohongshu, and other platforms without paying for APIs.
   Risk notes: The project relies on third-party tools and browser cookies for some platforms (Twitter, Reddit, Xiaohongshu), which carries account ban risk if detected. Some backends may break when platforms update their anti-scraping measures. The project's long-term maintenance depends on the author's continued engagement.
   LLM confidence: high

No single external trigger is identified, but the acceleration pattern suggests a catalyst such as a product launch or social media buzz.
   Target users: Software developers using VS Code, JetBrains, or CLI who want an AI coding agent with model flexibility, open-source transparency, and autonomous CI/CD capabilities.
   Risk notes: The project has 798 open issues, which may indicate growing pains or unresolved bugs. As a fork of OpenCode, its long-term maintainability and differentiation depend on the Kilo-Org team's commitment. Competition from established tools like Copilot and Cursor is intense.

Model & Demo Signals
1. How Transparent is DiffusionGemma?
   Source: arxiv | Type: paper
   URL: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.20560v1
   Signal captured
   Category: cs.LG
   Summary: LLM reasoning transparency is a critical affordance for understanding model decisions, mitigating misuse and misalignment, and debugging surprising model behaviors. However, DiffusionGemma performs a larger fraction of its computation in a continuous latent space; does this make its reasoning less transparent? We study this question by decomposing transparency into two components: variable transparency, whether we understand intermediate snapshots of a model's computational state; and algorithmic transparency, whether we can use these snapshots to reconstruct the process by which the model arrived at its outputs. Naively, DiffusionGemma has poor variable transparency: its opaque serial depth, the amount of serial computation that occurs in between interpretable model states, seems at first 28.6X higher than the corresponding autoregressive Gemma 4 model. However, we show that we can map the information flowing between denoising steps through an interpretable token bottleneck with no decrease in downstream performance. Treating these intermediate states as interpretable reduces the opaque serial depth to just 1.1X that of Gemma 4. Algorithmic transparency is harder for diffusion models than for autoregressive models because all token predictions in the canvas can change at every denoising step, giving the model the power to implement complicated distributed algorithms during the denoising process. To begin bridging this gap, we conduct a suite of interpretability case studies, uncovering initial evidence of novel diffusion-specific phenomena such as non-chronological reasoning, token and sequence smearing, and intermediate-context reasoning. Finally, we test monitorability, a key application of transparency that measures whether model outputs are useful for downstream tasks. We find that DiffusionGemma is similarly monitorable to Gemma 4.
   Why it matters: Matched AI developer keywords: llm, alignment

2. UNIEGO: Proxies as Mediators for Unified Egocentric Video Representation Learning
   Source: arxiv | Type: paper
   URL: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.20559v1
   Signal captured
   Category: cs.CV
   Summary: Egocentric video understanding is inherently limited by the narrow perspective of wearable cameras: a single viewpoint, a single modality, a single model cannot capture the full richness of human action. We argue that a truly expressive egocentric representation must subsume complementary knowledge across viewpoints, modalities, and foundation model representations, yet remain deployable from egocentric video alone. To this end, we introduce a hierarchical multi-teacher distillation framework that produces UNIEGO, a unified egocentric encoder trained with nine teachers spanning ego-exo viewpoints, RGB, depth, and skeleton modalities, and four foundation models. Rather than distilling directly from heterogeneous teachers whose incompatible architectures and feature geometries induce conflicting gradients, our framework interposes a layer of representation-specific Proxy models that translate diverse teacher knowledge into a homogeneous egocentric space. A second distillation stage, Selective Proxy Distillation (SPD), then adaptively selects, for each training sample, the subset of proxies that are both correct and confident, distilling exclusively from reliable supervision and suppressing erroneous signals. SPD is further stabilized by initializing UNIEGO as a learned convex combination of proxy parameters, placing the unified model in a well-conditioned region of the loss landscape before distillation begins. UNIEGO achieves state-of-the-art performance across three egocentric video understanding tasks - action recognition, video retrieval, and action segmentation on three challenging ego-exo benchmarks, outperforming naive multi-teacher distillation baselines and demonstrating that structured, proxy-mediated knowledge transfer yields richer and more discriminative egocentric representations.
   Why it matters: Matched AI developer keywords: benchmark

3. Toward Calibrated Mixture-of-Experts Under Distribution Shift
   Source: arxiv | Type: paper
   URL: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.20544v1
   Signal captured
   Category: cs.AI
   Summary: Calibration aligns a model's predictive uncertainty with the frequencies of its empirical outcomes and is important for understanding and trusting reported probabilities. Recent work shows that enforcing calibration at the level of individual predictors can improve ensemble accuracy and calibration, with mixture-of-experts (MoE) models showing strong empirical improvements in particular; however, the conditions under which calibration helps MoE are not well understood. In this work, we study how MoE models behave under distribution shift, focusing on how routing mechanisms interact with expert-level calibration. We show that expert calibration is sufficient to ensure calibration of the overall model under a broad class of distribution shifts in hard-routed models, but is insufficient for calibrating soft-routed models. To address this, we propose an adversarial reweighting that penalizes calibration errors of the routed aggregate under distribution shift, and we demonstrate that it improves the accuracy-calibration tradeoff both on average and on difficult subsets of the data, across model classes, prediction tasks, and distribution shifts.
   Why it matters: Matched AI developer keywords: rag

4. Multi-Task Bayesian In-Context Learning
   Source: arxiv | Type: paper
   URL: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.20538v1
   Signal captured
   Category: cs.LG
   Summary: Bayesian predictive inference provides a principled framework for uncertainty quantification, data efficiency, and robust generalization. However, exact inference is often intractable, and scalable approximations may remain computationally expensive or require restrictive modeling assumptions that degrade predictive performance. Prior-Data Fitted and in-context models have recently emerged as an amortized alternative by learning to map datasets directly to predictive distributions, but existing approaches are tightly coupled to the support of the training prior and lack explicit mechanisms for adapting to new priors at test time, resulting in limited robustness under distribution shift. We introduce a multi-task in-context learning framework for amortized hierarchical Bayesian predictive inference that explicitly represents prior information as a prefix of in-context datasets. A transformer trained on sequences of prior and target tasks learns to adapt its predictions across families of priors. On a suite of evaluations with increasing difficulty, including out-of-meta-distribution priors and priors with high-dimensional latent structures, our method matches oracle Bayesian predictors while being orders of magnitude faster. We further demonstrate its practical relevance on a real-world spatiotemporal temperature prediction benchmark. Code is available at https://github.com/martianmartina/multi-task-bayesian-icl/.
   Why it matters: Matched AI developer keywords: benchmark, evaluation

5. Execution-State Capsules: Graph-Bound Execution-State Checkpoint and Restore for Low-Latency, Small-Batch, On-Device Physical-AI Serving
   Source: arxiv | Type: paper
   URL: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.20537v1
   Signal captured
   Category: cs.LG
   Summary: Mainstream LLM serving systems reuse prefix work mainly through paged or radix key-value (KV) caches. This is highly effective for high-throughput, high-concurrency serving, but it manages only one positional fragment of execution state: the KV cache. We study the opposite regime: low-latency, small-batch, on-device physical-AI serving, where interactive LLM agents, speech systems, and robot policies repeatedly branch, reset, interrupt, and re-enter under tight responsiveness budgets. We introduce execution-state capsules, a graph-bound checkpoint and restore mechanism for the complete restorable state at a committed boundary. FlashRT is a white-box, backend-facing kernel runtime whose evaluated NVIDIA CUDA backend runs captured graph plans over contiguous static buffers with no block-table indirection. Because the live state is a closed set of named buffers, a capsule can snapshot, restore, fork, or roll back the whole execution boundary, including KV, recurrent state, convolution state, MTP state, and metadata. This moves reuse from token-addressed KV fragments to graph-bound execution-state boundaries. On an RTX 5090, capsule restore is byte-exact at the stored-state level and token-identical under greedy decode. A KV-only ablation diverges, showing that recurrent state is load-bearing. GPU-resident snapshot and restore are sub-millisecond, and TTFT speedup over cold prefill grows from 3.9x at 2k tokens to 27x at 16k tokens. On Jetson AGX Thor and DGX Spark, the same correctness and structural properties hold. Capsules are not a replacement for high-throughput KV-cache serving; they define a complementary latency-first serving point for explicit execution-state reuse.
   Why it matters: Matched AI developer keywords: llm, agent, rag

1. AI中心的数据黑洞
   Source: aihot | Type: curated_trend
   URL: https://www.dwarkesh.com/p/the-sample-efficiency-black-hole-2
   Signal captured
   Category: tools
   Summary: 智能的一种定义是样本效率，但近年AI进步主要靠扩充数据分布和增加算力。强化学习本质是合成数据生成--投入大量算力通过验证器筛选"好"数据，再训练模型预测正确输出。这一过程需要每个领域和技能的海量人类专家示例，数据行业年收入已达数十亿美元。近日Epoch报告，开源模型仅落后前沿闭源模型4个月，原因在于数据可从公开API蒸馏，而超参数等不易复制。人类一生接触约2亿token，前沿模型训练在数十到数百T token之间，相差近百万倍--机器人、自动驾驶等领域同样存在巨大效率差距。

14. MosaicLeaks： 你的研究智能体能保守秘密吗？
   Source: aihot | Type: curated_trend
   URL: https://huggingface.co/blog/ServiceNow/mosaicleaks
   Signal captured
   Category: papers
   Summary: 深度研究智能体在结合私有本地文档与外部网页检索时存在隐私泄露风险。MosaicLeaks 提出包含 1，001 条多跳研究链的新任务，每条链交错混合本地与公共子问题。测试发现智能体频繁泄露私有信息，单纯优化任务性能反而加剧泄露。基于此，研究提出隐私感知深度研究（PA-DR）强化学习训练方法，将严格链成功率从 48.7% 提升至 58.7%，同时将答案/全面信息泄露率从 34.0% 降至 9.9%。

15. OpenAI 强化学习实现广泛且持久的有益模型
   Source: aihot | Type: curated_trend
   URL: https://alignment.openai.com/beneficial-rl
   Signal captured
   Category: papers
   Summary: OpenAI 通过强化学习在真实对话场景中训练模型，使其展现诚实、认知谦逊、元认知透明、可纠正性、普遍公平性和对人类福祉的关心等有益特质。训练数据涵盖健康、教育、科学、法律、工程等多个领域。训练后模型在数十项独立对齐评测（包括奖励黑客、欺骗、有害建议、规范遵从等）上均表现提升，且这种改善泛化到未参与训练的领域、任务和评分设定。在对抗性提示或微调下，模型仍难以被导向有害行为，表明有益特质强化学习可产生广泛且持久的对齐泛化。
   Original: OpenAI：Alignment 研究博客（RSS） - https://alignment.openai.com/beneficial-rl
   Why it matters: OpenAI 通过强化学习在真实对话场景中训练模型，使其展现诚实、认知谦逊、元认知透明、可纠正性、普遍公平性和对人类福祉的关心等有益特质。训练数据涵盖健康、教育、科学、法律、工程等多个领域。训练后模型在数十项独立对齐评测（包括奖励黑客、欺骗、有害建议、规范遵从等）上均表现提升，且这种改善泛化到未参与训练的领域、任务和评分设定。在对抗性提示或微调下，模型仍难以被导向有害行为，表明有益特质强化学习可产生广泛且持久的对齐泛化。

数据说明：
- GitHub API 只提供当前 stars，总量变化来自本项目保存的历史 snapshot。
- daily delta 使用约 24h 前 snapshot，weekly delta 使用约 7 天前 snapshot。
- Accelerating 只展示至少 3 天历史基线且今日 delta 超过前 3 日均值 2 倍的项目。
- Potential Score 为规则评分，用于排序，不代表项目质量结论。
